United States.text:
As tournament hosts ranked 16th in the world, the United States carry the automatic boost of home advantage, and the model reflects it: a 47.5% chance to win their group and an 87% chance to clear the round of 32. From there the curve steepens — 56% to reach the last 16, 26.9% to the quarter-finals, and just 1.6% to lift the trophy on home soil. With a squad valued at €385.65m, this is a roster built to go deep, but the model treats anything beyond the quarters as a genuine long shot.

The recent form is the worry. The last 10 tell a split story: comfortable home wins over Uruguay (5-1), Japan and Paraguay sit alongside heavy defeats to Belgium (2-5), Portugal (0-2) and, most recently, a 1-2 loss to Germany in June. That run against elite European opposition is a flashing caution light heading into the finals. The talent is there across a settled defensive spine of Ream, Richards and McKenzie, but if the USMNT are to validate that 56% knockout figure, the results against the better sides have to start matching the friendlies they win at a canter.

Canada.text:
As one of the three hosts, Canada arrives ranked 30th in the world with a squad valued at €198.65m, and the model treats home advantage seriously: it makes them favourites to win their group at 56.75% and gives them a 97.74% chance of clearing the opening round into the last 32. From there the path narrows — 63.1% to reach the round of 16, 27.05% to the quarter-finals, and just 8.68% to a semi. The dream of lifting the trophy sits at 0.73%, which is roughly what you'd expect of a solid but not elite side carried by familiar faces.

The recent form reads quietly. Across their last ten, all but one at home, Canada won four, drew five and lost only once, a 0-1 to Australia last October. The defensive shape looks dependable — six clean sheets in that run, including goalless draws with Colombia, Ecuador and Tunisia — but the goals have been scarce, with five straight matches yielding two or fewer. A back line built around Johnston, Laryea and Cornelius keeps them in games; whether they can score enough to justify that 27% quarter-final number is the open question.

Mexico.text:
Mexico arrive as hosts ranked 15th in the world, and the recent form backs the billing: their last ten have produced seven wins, including a 5-1 dismantling of Serbia and a 4-0 over Iceland, with goalless draws against Portugal and Uruguay showing the defence can hold its shape against quality. The only blemish is a 1-2 home loss to Paraguay back in November. With a squad valued at €191.85m and three experienced goalkeepers led by veteran G. Ochoa, this is a settled, home-comfortable side carrying genuine momentum into the tournament.

The model likes the platform but not the ceiling. It makes Mexico clear favourites to win their group at 57.96% and gives them a 56.88% chance of reaching the round of 16, but the numbers thin quickly after that: 23.7% to make the quarter-finals, 7.85% to the semis, and just 0.74% to lift the trophy. That gap is the honest story here — host advantage and form should carry Mexico into the knockouts comfortably, but the model sees them as a team likelier to be a quarter-final wall than a champion, and the deep run remains very much an open question.

Panama.text:
Panama arrive ranked 33rd in the world, a CONCACAF side whose qualifying form was solid rather than spectacular: a 3-0 win over El Salvador and a 3-2 result away to Guatemala in November, either side of a 1-1 home draw with Suriname. The friendly run since has been mixed — a 4-2 win over the Dominican Republic and a 2-1 victory away to South Africa sit alongside a heavy 6-2 loss in Brazil and a 1-1 draw with Bosnia & Herzegovina. That's a team that can score against most opponents but has shown it can leak goals badly against elite attacks.

The model treats this as a tournament about survival, not silverware. Panama are given a 28.3% chance of reaching the round of 32 and just 3.79% of making the round of 16, with the quarter-finals (0.51%) effectively out of reach and a 1.67% shot at winning their group. With a squad valued at €34.55m, those are honest numbers for a side that will likely need a result against stronger opposition to extend its stay. Getting out of the group would already count as a real success.

Argentina.text:
Argentina arrive as reigning world champions and the model treats them accordingly, ranking them third in the world and giving them an 18.9% shot at lifting the trophy again — the kind of number that makes them a clear favourite without being a foregone conclusion. The recent record backs it up: nine wins in their last ten, with friendly thrashings of Puerto Rico (6-0) and Zambia (5-0) and a tidy 2-0 over Honduras in June. The only blemish is a 0-1 loss to Ecuador in qualifying, a reminder that this side is beatable on an off night.

The path the simulations draw is comfortable early and steeper late. Argentina win their group 75.9% of the time and clear the round of 32 in 99.1% of runs, but the odds thin as the bracket tightens — 58.7% to reach the quarters, 43.5% to make the semis, and 28.7% to reach the final. With a squad valued at €782.5m and Emiliano Martínez behind a back line built around Lisandro Martínez, the spine is there; the question is whether it holds across seven knockout games rather than one good night.

Brazil.text:
Brazil arrive ranked sixth in the world with the most valuable squad in the field at €928.2m, and the model treats them accordingly: a 61.2% chance of winning their group and a 10.4% shot at lifting the trophy, the kind of number that marks them as a genuine contender without making them favourites. The path tells the story — 98.2% to clear the round of 32, 68.7% to reach the last 16, but only 31.3% to make a semi-final and 18.1% to reach the final. The deeper you go, the thinner the margin.

The recent form is harder to read than the price tag suggests. The headline results are emphatic — 6-2 over Panama, 5-0 away to South Korea — but mixed in are a 0-1 qualifying loss in Bolivia, a 2-3 defeat in Japan and a 1-2 home loss to France. Against the better sides, A seleção have looked beatable. With Alisson, Ederson and Weverton behind a back line built around Gabriel Magalhães and Bremer, the foundation is strong; the question the model keeps asking is whether it holds against elite opposition.

Colombia.text:
Colombia arrive ranked 13th in the world and carrying real momentum, with squad value around €302m and a recent run that says plenty. Their last ten games include a 6-3 win away to Venezuela in qualifying, a 4-0 drubbing of Mexico in Mexico, and warm-up wins over Jordan and Costa Rica. The only blemishes came against serious European opposition, back-to-back home defeats to Croatia (1-2) and France (1-3) in March, a reminder that the step up in class still bites. Los Cafeteros otherwise look hard to beat.

The model treats them as a solid but not elite side. It gives Colombia a 34.9% chance of winning their group and an 86.9% shot at reaching the round of 32, so survival into the knockouts is close to a formality. From there the odds thin out fast: 56.3% to make the last 16, 29.6% to reach the quarter-finals, and just 14.6% to play a semi. A run to the final sits at 6.7%, and the title at 2.8%, putting them firmly among the contenders without ever being favourites.

Uruguay.text:
Marcelo Bielsa's Uruguay arrive 17th in the world but in patchy form. The recent ledger is heavy on draws — goalless against Algeria, 1-1 with England away, a blank against Mexico — and punctuated by a chastening 1-5 loss to the USA in November. The qualifying campaign was sturdier, with a 3-0 win over Peru and 2-0 over Venezuela offsetting a 0-2 defeat in Paraguay. A squad worth €359.3m, anchored by defenders like Araújo and Giménez, has the names to compete; whether the goals arrive consistently is the open question the friendlies leave hanging.

The model treats La Celeste as a solid second-tier contender rather than a favourite. Surviving the group stage is close to a formality at 90.7%, but winning the group sits at just 22.3%, and the knockout odds thin quickly: 36.7% to reach the last 16, 20.8% to the quarters, and 10.6% to the semis. The numbers put a deep run within reach without making it likely — a 4.6% shot at the final and 1.8% to lift the trophy frame Uruguay as dangerous outsiders, not the field's measuring stick.

Ecuador.text:
Ecuador arrive in form that's hard to read cleanly: unbeaten across their last ten, but six of those were draws, including goalless stalemates against Canada and Paraguay and 1-1 results away to the Netherlands, Morocco, Mexico and the USA. The standout remains a 1-0 home win over Argentina in qualifying, and the recent friendly run — 3-0 over Guatemala, 2-1 over Saudi Arabia — suggests the goals are starting to flow again. This is a side built on a defensive spine, with Pacho, Hincapié, Estupiñán and Ordoñez anchoring a €368.7m squad ranked 23rd in the world.

The model treats them as a solid knockout proposition rather than a contender. It gives Ecuador a 89.7% chance of clearing the group's first cut and a near coin-flip 49.2% to reach the last 16, but only a 28.4% shot at winning their group. From there the odds thin quickly: 21.5% to make the quarters, 9.5% for the semis, and just 1.5% to lift the trophy. A team that frustrates good opposition and rarely loses, but one whose low-scoring habit caps the ceiling.

Paraguay.text:
Paraguay arrive ranked 40th in the world, a side whose pedigree — two Copa América titles, ten World Cups and a quarter-final run in 2010 — outstrips a modest squad valued at €153.65m. The qualifying campaign that brought them here was built on grit rather than goals: a 1-0 win in Peru, a goalless draw with Ecuador and a narrow loss in Brazil tell the story of a hard-to-beat team that keeps games tight. Recent friendlies have been mixed, a 4-0 dismantling of Nicaragua and an away win over Mexico sitting alongside defeats to Morocco, the USA and South Korea, so the form line is genuinely uneven heading into the tournament.

The model treats them as a credible but unspectacular outsider. It gives Paraguay a 52.9% chance of surviving the group stage and reaching the round of 32, but only a 12.1% shot at winning the group and a 20.6% chance of progressing to the last 16. From there the odds thin sharply — 5.5% to reach the quarter-finals, and just 0.07% to lift the trophy. A solid floor, in other words, with a low ceiling.

Germany.text:
Germany arrive in form: nine wins from their last ten, the only blemish a 0-2 trip to Slovakia in September that they answered emphatically with a 6-0 demolition of the same side in November. Recent friendlies have been productive too — 4-0 over Finland, 2-1 away at the USA — and the qualifying run was built on clean sheets and goals in bulk. Ranked tenth by FIFA and carrying a squad valued at €947m, this is a team that looks the part on paper and on the recent scoreline.

Yet the model is cooler than the reputation. It gives Germany a 96% chance of clearing the group stage and a 61.6% shot at reaching the last 16, but the numbers thin out fast after that: 30.3% to make the quarter-finals, 15.3% the semis, and just 6.7% to reach the final. Champion sits at only 2.7%, a reminder that strong form against qualifying opposition doesn't translate into favouritism in a deep field. A side this experienced should advance comfortably — going all the way is a different question entirely.

England.text:
England arrive ranked fourth in the world with a squad valued at €1.36bn, and the qualifying campaign backs up that billing: five wins from five in European qualification, including 5-0 routs of Latvia and Serbia, with clean sheets in every one. The model is duly impressed, making England 74% to win their group and an overwhelming 98.7% to clear the first knockout round. Champion sits at 10.5%, with reaching the final priced at 18% — respectable, but a reminder that even a top-four side is more likely than not to fall short of the last two.

The caveat is the friendly form. Since qualifying wrapped up, England have looked ordinary: a 1-1 draw with Uruguay, a 0-1 home loss to Japan and a narrow 1-0 over New Zealand, on top of a 1-3 defeat to Senegal last June. The model's confidence rests heavily on the competitive results, not these warm-ups, so the gap between a ruthless qualifier and a stuttering friendly side is the real open question. Get past the quarter-final, which the model rates at 51%, and the deeper run becomes plausible.

France.text:
France arrive as the world's number one side, and the model treats them with appropriate respect: an 8.2% chance of lifting the trophy, a 15.6% shot at reaching the final, and a 56.2% likelihood of winning their group. Survival is all but assumed — they clear the round of 32 in 95.9% of simulations — but the path narrows fast, with the quarter-final reached only 46% of the time and the semis just 28.1%. That is the shape of a genuine contender rather than a runaway favourite. A squad valued at €1.52 billion, anchored by Maignan in goal and a defence stacked with Konaté and the Hernández brothers, gives the projection its floor.

The form behind the numbers is mostly convincing without being flawless. Les Bleus closed qualifying strongly, beating Ukraine 4-0 at home and winning in Azerbaijan, and the spring friendlies brought away wins over Brazil (2-1) and Colombia (3-1). But the 1-2 home loss to Ivory Coast in June is the sort of result that explains why the model stops short of crowning them, even as it ranks them among the very best.

Spain.text:
Ranked second in the world and carrying a squad valued at €1.22 billion, Spain arrive in form. Their qualifying campaign was emphatic: five wins from six, including a 6-0 demolition of Türkiye away and three more clean-sheet victories over Bulgaria and Georgia, with only a 2-2 home draw against Türkiye to blot the record. The warm-up friendlies were patchier — a 3-1 win in Peru either side of a 1-1 draw with Iraq and a goalless stalemate against Egypt — but a 3-0 win over Serbia in March suggests the firepower is still there when it matters.

The model likes them. Spain are given a 74.99% chance of winning their group and a 69.66% shot at reaching the last 16, with the quarter-finals close to a coin flip at 50.8%. From there the odds thin, as you would expect: 37.64% to make the semis, 24.41% to reach the final and 14.26% to lift the trophy — the kind of numbers that mark them as genuine contenders without making them favourites. With three credible goalkeepers and a defence built around Cubarsí, Laporte and Cucurella, the foundation looks solid.

Portugal.text:
Roberto Martínez's Portugal arrive ranked fifth in the world with a squad valued at just over a billion euros, and the model treats them as genuine contenders without quite believing in a coronation. It gives them a 53.9% chance of winning their group, a 69% shot at reaching the last 16 and a 43.9% chance of making the quarter-finals, but only a 6.5% chance of lifting the trophy — respectable, yet some way behind the favourites. The pieces are there, from Diogo Costa in goal to a defence built around Rúben Dias, João Cancelo and Diogo Dalot, all marshalled by captain Cristiano Ronaldo.

The recent form reads well on balance but carries a warning. Portugal won six of their last ten and battered Armenia 9-1, yet that run also includes a 0-2 qualifying defeat away to the Republic of Ireland and draws against Hungary and Mexico. That mix of demolitions and stumbles is why the model stops short of the top bracket: the 24% semi-final and 12.8% final figures say a deep run is plausible, not expected. Treat them as a strong side with a ceiling, not a certainty.

Netherlands.text:
The seventh-ranked side in the world arrives with a €754.2m squad built around a defensive spine — Van Dijk, Aké, Timber and Dumfries — and a goalkeeping queue three deep in Flekken, Roefs and Verbruggen. Qualifying was largely comfortable: three 4-0 wins over Lithuania, Finland and Malta, with the only blemishes a pair of 1-1 draws against Poland. The friendly form is harder to read. Since then the Dutch beat Norway and Uzbekistan but also lost 0-1 at home to Algeria and were held 1-1 by Ecuador, so the warm-up phase raised as many questions as it settled.

The model treats the Netherlands as a strong qualifier rather than a favourite. It makes them clear to reach the round of 32 at 90%, and the most likely group winners at 43%, but the curve steepens fast after that: a 42% chance at the last 16, 25% to reach the quarter-finals and just 12.7% to make a semi. A final appearance sits at 5.7% and the title at 2.4%. In short, a side good enough to go deep, but priced as an outsider for the trophy.

Belgium.text:
Belgium arrive ranked ninth in the world and carrying a squad valued at €547.5m, and the recent form behind those numbers is emphatic. The build-up to the tournament was a procession: 5-0 over Tunisia, 2-0 away to Croatia, and a 5-2 win in the United States, all on the back of a European qualifying run that produced 7-0 and two 6-0 thrashings. The blemishes were draws, not defeats — 1-1 with Mexico, 1-1 away to Kazakhstan, 0-0 at home to North Macedonia — which hint at a side that overwhelms weaker opponents but can be frustrated by organised ones.

The model likes them to a point. It makes Belgium clear favourites to win their group at 63%, with reaching the last 32 close to a formality at 94% and the round of 16 a 65% proposition. After that the numbers cool quickly: 36% to make the quarter-finals, 15% the semis, and just 2.8% to lift the trophy. With Courtois behind a relatively young defence, this reads as a team built to go deep without being a genuine title pick.

Austria.text:
Austria arrive in good order. They lost just once in their last ten — a 0-1 trip to Romania last October — and have otherwise piled up wins, including a 10-0 demolition of San Marino and a tidy 2-1 away result against Bosnia & Herzegovina. The three 2026 friendlies all went their way, narrow 1-0s over Tunisia and South Korea bracketing a 5-1 rout of Ghana. Ranked 24th by FIFA with a squad valued at €242.2m and built around the experience of Alaba, Danso and Friedl at the back, this is a side that defends well and finds enough at the other end.

The model likes them to get out of the blocks but not much further. It gives Austria a 70.9% chance of clearing the group stage into the round of 32, yet only a 21.3% shot at reaching the last 16 and an 8.4% chance of the quarter-finals. Winning the group sits at just 7.96%, and the deeper runs thin out fast — 2.6% to a semi-final, 0.2% to lift the trophy. In short, a solid tournament floor with a low ceiling.

Switzerland.text:
Switzerland arrive ranked 19th in the world, and the route here was solid: their European qualifying campaign produced wins over Sweden home and away (4-1 and 2-0), Slovenia and Kosovo, with only draws at Slovenia and Kosovo to blemish it. The friendly form since is murkier — a 4-1 dismissal of Jordan, but also a 1-1 against Australia, a goalless draw in Norway, and a 4-3 home loss to Germany that exposed the back line. With a squad valued at €332.5m and led by defender Manuel Akanji, this is a side built to grind rather than overwhelm.

The model treats them as a near-lock to escape the group stage at 95.5% to reach the round of 32, and rates them roughly even money to make the last 16 at 55.3%. From there the air thins fast: a 20% shot at the quarter-finals, 6.9% at the semis, and just 0.63% to lift the trophy. That feels about right for a team good enough to trouble anyone on its day but, on the Germany evidence, leaky enough to be undone by it too.

Turkey.text:
Turkey arrive in good rhythm, winning seven of their last ten and carrying a run of six straight victories into the tournament, including tight 1-0 qualifying wins over Romania and Kosovo and a 4-0 friendly rout of FYR Macedonia. The blot on the record is Spain, who took both honours emphatically: a 6-0 humbling in Seville's reverse fixture sense back in September, softened only by a 2-2 draw away in November. Ranked 22nd by FIFA with a squad valued at €473.7m, this is a side that beats everyone below the elite and has yet to prove it can live with the very top.

The model reads them as a solid knockout side rather than a contender. It gives Turkey a 64.8% chance to clear the group stage and a 30.1% shot at the last 16, but only 19.4% to win their group outright. From there the odds thin quickly: 9.6% to reach the quarter-finals, 2.9% for the semis, and just 0.2% to lift the trophy. A deep run is plausible; anything beyond the quarters would be a genuine overachievement.

Croatia.text:
Croatia arrive ranked 11th in the world, their qualifying campaign a model of efficiency: seven wins and a goalless draw away to the Czech Republic, with Montenegro beaten home and away and Faroe Islands twice seen off. The squad, valued at €387.3m, leans on a settled defensive spine in Josko Gvardiol, Josip Stanisic and Dejan Lovren's heirs at the back, plus a goalkeeping rotation of Livakovic, Kotarski and Pandur. The recent friendlies told a more sobering story — a 0-2 home loss to Belgium and a 1-3 defeat in Brazil sandwiching narrow wins over Colombia and Slovenia — a reminder that the step up from qualifying minnows to genuine contenders is steep.

Our model reads them as a solid knockout side rather than a title threat. They reach the round of 32 in 87% of simulations and clear the round of 16 in 39%, but the projection thins fast: a 14.5% quarter-final, 5.6% semi-final and just a 0.5% chance of lifting the trophy. Winning the group sits at 20.6%, so a tougher draw and an early test look the likeliest path through.

Scotland.text:
Scotland arrive ranked 43rd in the world, qualifying through a European campaign that ended on a high: a 4-2 win over Denmark and a 3-1 home result against Greece bookended a run that included a goalless draw away in Copenhagen and two wins over Belarus. The form since has been streaky — back-to-back 1-0 home defeats to Japan and Ivory Coast in March, then 4-0 and 4-1 friendly routs of Bolivia and Curaçao. A squad valued at €170.25m leans on experience, with three goalkeepers in Gordon, Gunn and Kelly and a defensive core built around Hanley, Hendry, Hickey, Hyam and McKenna.

The model is sober about the ceiling. It gives Scotland a 42.5% chance of reaching the round of 32 but only 10.6% to make the last 16, and just 2.8% to win their group outright. Beyond that the odds thin out fast — 2.5% to reach a quarter-final, 0.5% a semi, and a rounding-error 0.01% to lift the trophy. The realistic question here is survival past the group stage, where the numbers say it's closer to a coin-toss-and-a-half against.

Morocco.text:
Morocco arrive ranked eighth in the world and carrying real momentum. They lifted the African Cup of Nations earlier this year on the back of a clean knockout run — beating Zambia, Cameroon, Nigeria and Senegal without conceding much of note — and the friendly results since have followed the same line, with 4-0 and 5-0 wins over Madagascar and Burundi either side of a 1-1 draw with Norway. A squad valued at €498.3m and built around familiar names like Bounou, Aguerd and Hakimi gives the Atlas Lions both depth and a settled spine.

The model likes them without crowning them. It makes Morocco 94.4% to clear the group stage and 34.5% to win the group outright, with a 56.7% chance of reaching the last 16. From there the odds thin in the usual way — 36.5% to make the quarter-finals, 20.3% for the semis, and 4.8% to go all the way. That champion number puts them in the second tier of contenders rather than the favourites, which feels about right for a side that does the hard defensive work well but still has to prove it over seven games.

Senegal.text:
Senegal arrive ranked 14th in the world and carrying a squad valued at €478.1m, depth that shows in three goalkeepers of standing in Mike Diaw, Yehvann Diouf and Édouard Mendy, and a defensive spine built around Kalidou Koulibaly and Moussa Niakhaté. The recent form reads well: the Lions reached the Africa Cup of Nations through a strong run that included a 3-0 win over Botswana, away wins at Benin and Mali, and a 1-0 victory over Egypt, before a 0-3 defeat to Morocco ended their tournament. Friendly wins over Peru and Gambia followed, though a 2-3 loss in the USA is the most recent result.

The model treats Senegal as a solid knockout side rather than a favourite. It gives them an 83.5% chance to clear the group stage and a 46.9% shot at the round of 16, but the numbers thin quickly thereafter: 21.5% to reach the quarter-finals, 9.4% to make the semis, and just 1.4% to lift the trophy. Winning their group is rated at 22.7%, so first place is far from assumed.

Egypt.text:
Egypt arrive ranked 29th in the world with a squad valued at €116.48m, and the model treats them as a side likely to survive the expanded group stage but no further. It gives the Pharaohs a 68.3% chance of reaching the round of 32, but only 27.4% of making the round of 16 and 15.5% of actually winning their group. Beyond that the numbers thin out fast: 7.6% to reach the quarter-finals, and a 0.12% shot at lifting the trophy. This is the profile of a solid qualifier rather than a contender.

Recent form reads as steady without being commanding. The friendly results are encouraging — a 1-0 win over Russia, a goalless draw away in Spain, a 4-0 thrashing of Saudi Arabia — and even the 1-2 defeat to Brazil came against far stronger opposition. The Africa Cup of Nations campaign was patchier, with wins over South Africa, Benin and Ivory Coast undone by defeats to Senegal and Nigeria. With four goalkeepers and a defence-heavy core listed among the key names, Egypt look built to grind out results rather than overwhelm anyone.

Ivory Coast.text:
The Elephants arrive in 2026 in genuinely good form, unbeaten in their last five and beating France 2-1 away in June, with earlier friendly wins over Scotland and a 4-0 thrashing of South Korea. That run followed an Africa Cup of Nations campaign that ended with a 2-3 loss to Egypt but featured wins over Burkina Faso and Gabon. This is a third African title-winning generation, ranked 34th in the world with a squad valued at €522.1m, and a defensive spine of Kossounou, Diomande, Agbadou and Konan that has kept clean sheets against serious opposition.

The model treats Ivory Coast as a solid knockout side rather than a contender. It gives them an 85.9% chance of clearing the group stage and a 41.9% shot at the round of 16, but only 22.3% to win their group, so the path likely runs through a runner-up finish. From there the odds thin quickly: 16.4% to reach the quarter-finals, 6.3% for the semis, and just 0.75% to lift the trophy. A deep run is plausible on current form; winning it all would be a genuine surprise.

South Africa.text:
Bafana Bafana arrive ranked 60th in the world and carry one of the leaner squads in the field, valued at just €49.25m, and the recent form does little to suggest a side about to punch above that. Their last ten tell a story of stalemates and narrow margins: draws against Jamaica, Nicaragua and twice with Panama, a group-stage Africa Cup of Nations run that ended in defeats to Egypt and Cameroon around wins over Angola and Zimbabwe. The goals have dried up at the wrong moments, and South Africa look more like a side that grinds out 1-1s than one that breaks games open.

The model reads it the same way. It gives South Africa a 33.97% chance of reaching the round of 32 — better than a coin flip against, but hardly comfortable — and only a 4.23% shot at topping their group. Beyond that the door shuts quickly: 7.33% to make the round of 16, 1.07% to reach a quarter-final, and effectively nothing past that. This is a tournament about survival rather than ambition, and even survival is no better than an even-money bet.

DR Congo.text:
The Leopards arrive ranked 46th, having booked their place through the intercontinental play-offs with a 1-0 win over Jamaica, and they got there on a genuinely solid run. Across their last ten they've lost only once — 0-1 to Algeria at the Africa Cup of Nations — while holding Denmark scoreless, beating Botswana 3-0 and edging Benin and Cameroon away. A squad worth €143.9m, anchored by a deep goalkeeping group and defenders like Kalulu, Batubinsika and Masuaku, looks built to frustrate opponents, which fits the low-scoring, clean-sheet pattern in those recent results.

The model is more cautious than the form suggests. It gives DR Congo a 40.9% chance of escaping the group into the round of 32, but only 12.4% to reach the round of 16 and 3.3% to make the quarter-finals. Winning their group is rated at just 5.2%, and the path beyond thins quickly — 0.85% to a semi-final, 0.04% to lift the trophy. In short, this is a side the numbers expect to be competitive for a place in the knockouts, but a clear underdog to go deep once there.

Tunisia.text:
Tunisia arrive ranked 44th with a squad valued at just under €70m, and their recent form does little to inspire. The Eagles of Carthage closed their warm-up schedule with heavy friendly defeats, beaten 5-0 by Belgium and 1-0 by Austria, after a goalless draw with Canada. Their Africa Cup of Nations campaign was equally flat — a 3-1 win over Uganda aside, they lost to Nigeria, drew with Tanzania and fell to Mali, never quite stringing results together. The brighter notes came earlier, at the Arab Cup, where a 3-0 win over Qatar hinted at what this side can do when it clicks.

The model sees a tough road. Tunisia are given a 39.1% chance of reaching the round of 32 and only a 5.3% shot at winning their group, with the knockout picture thinning fast from there: 8.9% to make the round of 16, 2.4% to reach the quarter-finals, and essentially nothing — 0.02% — to lift the trophy. On this evidence, simply progressing from the group would count as a solid return for a side short on recent momentum.

Japan.text:
Japan arrive in form that demands attention. They've won eight of their last ten, and the names on the wrong end of those scorelines carry weight: 1-0 away at England in March, the same margin at Scotland three days earlier, and a 3-2 home win over Brazil last October. The only blemishes are a 0-2 loss in the United States and goalless and 2-2 draws in Mexico and at home to Paraguay. Ranked 18th in the world with a squad valued at €270.85m, this is a side built on a settled defensive shape, conceding rarely across that run.

The model likes them without loving them. It makes Japan 90.1% to clear their first match and 43.4% to win their group, but the path narrows quickly: 43.3% to reach the last 16, 26.5% to make the quarter-finals, and just 2.9% to lift the trophy. That gap between near-certain qualification and a thin championship number is the honest read on a team that beats anyone on its day but hasn't yet shown it can string those days together over a knockout run. The wins over England and Brazil say the ceiling is real.

South Korea.text:
South Korea arrive 25th in the world and carry a squad valued at €139m, and the model treats them as a side likely to survive but unlikely to go deep. The numbers tell a consistent story: 80.8% to clear the round of 32, 36.5% to reach the round of 16, but only 11.2% to make the quarter-finals and 0.22% to lift the trophy. Winning the group is rated at 26.1%, so an early knockout exit looks like the base case rather than a disappointment.

The form line explains the caution. Korea have beaten the teams they should — 5-0 over Trinidad and Tobago, 2-0 wins away at the USA and over Paraguay, and recent 1-0 results against El Salvador and Ghana — but they have been outclassed when the level rises, losing 0-5 to Brazil and 0-4 to Ivory Coast at home and 0-1 to Austria away. That gap between comfortable wins over modest opponents and heavy defeats to stronger ones is exactly why the model has them clearing the first hurdle but stalling soon after. Kim Min-Jae anchors a defence that will need to hold up far better than those scorelines suggest.

Iran.text:
Iran arrive ranked 21st in the world but with a squad valued at just €32.05m, one of the leaner groups at the tournament, leaning on experienced names like goalkeeper Beiranvand and defenders Hajisafi and Rezaeian. Recent form points up: they closed their preparation with home wins over Mali (2-0) and Gambia (3-1) and a thumping 5-0 at Costa Rica in March. The wider run is patchier, though — defeats to Nigeria, Russia and Uzbekistan, plus goalless friendlies against Cape Verde and Uzbekistan, suggest a side that can grind out results without always creating freely.

The model treats them as a likely qualifier but no further. It gives Iran a 66.4% chance of surviving the group-stage cut into the round of 32, but only 25.6% to reach the last 16 and 6.8% to make a quarter-final. Winning the group sits at 15.1%, and anything deeper looks remote: 1.7% for a semi-final and 0.09% to lift the trophy. In short, the numbers frame Iran as a team built to reach the knockouts and then run into the tournament's sharper edges.

Australia.text:
Australia arrive ranked 27th with one of the lighter squads in the field at €77.45m, and a recent run that swings between encouraging and flat. The FIFA Series in March was the high point — a 1-0 win over Cameroon followed by a 5-1 rout of Curaçao — but either side of that the friendlies tell a harder story: 0-3 in Colombia, 0-1 to Venezuela, 0-1 to Mexico, and a 1-1 draw with Switzerland in June. The recent wins over New Zealand and Canada keep the picture mixed rather than alarming, but the goals have largely dried up against stronger opposition.

The model reads them as a side likely to reach the knockouts and unlikely to go much further. It gives Australia a 68% chance of clearing the group stage and a 21% shot at winning the group, but the odds taper quickly after that — 34% to reach the last 16, 11% to the quarter-finals, and just 3.7% to a semi-final. Champion at 0.3% is a number to admire rather than back. The route is plausible; the ceiling looks firmly set.

Saudi Arabia.text:
Saudi Arabia arrive ranked 61st in the world and as one of the lighter squads in the field, valued at just €40.68m. Recent form does little to inspire: the Falcons have lost five of their last ten, including a 0-4 home thrashing by Egypt and narrow defeats to Serbia and Ecuador, though a 3-0 win over Puerto Rico in early June at least steadied things before the tournament. The Arab Cup run in December was much the same story — wins over Comoros, Oman and Palestine sat alongside losses to Morocco and Jordan. This is a team that beats lesser sides and struggles against anyone better.

The model sees that pattern clearly. It gives Saudi Arabia just a 30.7% chance of escaping the group into the round of 32, and only 4.1% to reach the last 16. Winning the group is treated as remote at 1.2%, and the deeper stages — a 0.1% semi-final chance, no realistic title path at all — barely register. The honest read is a side hoping to ride the expanded format into a knockout place, with everything beyond that a long shot.

Uzbekistan.text:
Uzbekistan arrive at their first World Cup ranked 50th in the world and carrying a squad valued at a modest €85.33m, the kind of profile that makes them clear underdogs rather than dark horses. The model agrees: it gives them just a 6 percent chance of winning their group and 44 percent of surviving to the round of 32, with the odds thinning quickly after that — 14 percent to reach the last 16, under 4 percent to make a quarter-final, and a rounding-error 0.04 percent to lift the trophy. This is a side the numbers expect to be tested for survival, not asked to chase deep runs.

The recent form offers a more encouraging read than the rankings. Across their last ten they lost only to higher-grade opposition — Netherlands, Canada and Uruguay — while keeping clean sheets in goalless draws with Iran and Venezuela and beating Egypt 2-0. The defence looks the backbone here, with Khusanov, Ashurmatov and a deep goalkeeping pool, but the friendly defeats to Canada and the Netherlands hint at the gap to the level they will meet.

Jordan.text:
Jordan arrive ranked 63rd with one of the leaner squads in the field at €20.3m, and the recent form tells two stories. The Al Nashama were excellent at the 2025 Arab Cup, stringing together five straight wins including 3-0 at Egypt and 1-0 away to Saudi Arabia, before the run stalled with a 3-2 home loss to Morocco. The friendlies since have been sobering: a 2-2 home draw with Costa Rica, another with Nigeria, then back-to-back away defeats to Switzerland (1-4) and Colombia (0-2). Against stronger opposition, the defensive frailty is real.

The model is blunt about the ceiling. Jordan are given just a 0.59% chance of winning their group and a 14.2% chance of reaching the round of 32, with knockout football beyond that fading fast — 1.7% for the round of 16 and effectively nothing past the quarter-finals. That reads as a side that can be a nuisance, as the Arab Cup showed, but lacks the squad depth to grind through a World Cup group. Survival, not progression, is the realistic target here.

Qatar.text:
Qatar arrive at the World Cup with their form pointing the wrong way. The Maroons, ranked 55th, have won just once in their last ten outings — a 2-1 home win over the United Arab Emirates in qualifying — while shipping heavy defeats to Tunisia (0-3) and Russia (1-4) and going scoreless in five of those matches, including the most recent goalless friendly draw with El Salvador. With a squad valued at just €19.93m, the attacking threat looks thin, and the run through the December Arab Cup, where they lost to both Palestine and Tunisia, did little to suggest momentum is building.

The model reflects that. It gives Qatar only an 11.3% chance — barely one in nine — of even clearing the round of 32, and a 23.56% probability of going out at that first knockout hurdle. Beyond that the path narrows sharply: a 2.85% shot at the round of 16, 0.24% at the quarter-finals, and effectively no chance of lifting the trophy. On current evidence, simply being competitive in the group stage would represent a respectable showing for this side.

New Zealand.text:
New Zealand arrive as OFC's standard-bearers but ranked 85th in the world, and recent form does little to inspire. The last ten games yielded a single win — a 4-1 thumping of Chile in the March FIFA Series — against seven defeats, with the warm-up window especially grim: 0-4 to Haiti and 0-1 to England in June, sandwiching earlier losses to Australia, Poland and Ecuador. A 1-1 draw away to Norway is the only other bright spot. A squad valued at just €34.35m, light on the kind of names that move tournaments, frames the challenge plainly.

The model treats this as a survival story rather than a deep run. It gives New Zealand a 40.6% chance of reaching the round of 32 — better than a coin flip against, but the likeliest single outcome is going home early — and only a 10.8% shot at the last 16. Winning the group is a 6.4% long shot, and anything beyond the quarter-finals (1.9%) is effectively a rounding error, with the title priced at 0.01%. For this group, escaping the opening stage would already count as a real success.

Norway.text:
Norway arrive in their first World Cup since the late 1990s riding a qualifying campaign that flattered the scoreboard: 11-1 over Moldova, 5-0 against Israel, and a 4-1 win away to Italy that announced them as more than makeweights. The recent form is uneven away from the goal-fest — a 0-0 with Switzerland, a 1-2 loss in the Netherlands and a 1-1 draw in Morocco this June — but a 3-1 home win over Sweden kept the mood buoyant. Ranked 31st by FIFA and carrying a squad valued at 589.9 million euros under Ståle Solbakken, this is a side that scores freely but hasn't been fully tested at tournament level.

The model treats them as a solid knockout outfit rather than a contender. It gives Norway an 81 percent chance of clearing the group stage and 44 percent to reach the last 16, but the line steepens sharply after that: 19 percent to make the quarters, under 8 percent for the semis, and just 1 percent to lift the trophy. Win the group at 20 percent, and the path opens; finish second, and it narrows fast.

Sweden.text:
Sweden arrive ranked 38th in the world, carrying a €406m squad built around a settled defensive core in Lindelöf, Hien and Lagerbielke, yet the route here was anything but smooth. The autumn qualifiers were a near-collapse — back-to-back defeats to Kosovo, two losses to Switzerland including a 4-1 hammering, and a goalless home loss that left the campaign on the brink. The March turnaround told a different story: a 3-1 win away to Ukraine and a 3-2 victory over Poland restored some belief, before mixed friendlies, a 3-1 loss to Norway and a 2-2 draw with Greece, muddied the picture again.

The model reads that volatility plainly. It gives Sweden just an 8.3% chance of winning their group and a 49.8% shot at surviving the opening phase — essentially a coin flip to reach the knockouts at all. Beyond that the odds thin quickly: 12.7% to make the last 16, under 4% to reach a quarter-final, and a token 0.05% to lift the trophy. This is a side capable of beating anyone on its day, but the numbers say consistency, not ceiling, is the question.

Czech Republic.text:
Recognised by FIFA as Czechia, the Czech Republic arrive ranked 41st in the world and without the security blanket of home advantage. Their qualifying campaign was a study in not losing rather than dominating: 2-2 with the Republic of Ireland, 1-1 with Denmark, a 0-0 against Croatia and a 2-0 win in Montenegro all came alongside a 6-0 thrashing of Gibraltar. The blot was a 1-2 defeat away to the Faroe Islands in October, a result that still stings against the run of an otherwise unbeaten recent stretch that includes friendly wins over Kosovo and Guatemala in the build-up.

The model treats them as a side likely to survive the opening round but no further. It gives them a 60.8% chance of reaching the round of 32 and just an 11.7% shot at winning their group, with the knockout odds thinning fast: 19.3% to make the last 16, 4.2% to reach the quarters and only 0.03% to lift the trophy. With a squad valued at €188m and built on a deep goalkeeping pool, this is a team measured by its floor rather than its ceiling.

Bosnia and Herzegovina.text:
Bosnia and Herzegovina arrive ranked 65th in the world and valued at a modest €151.6m, and the model treats them accordingly: a near coin-flip 40.4% to survive the group stage into the round of 32, but only 7% to reach the round of 16 and 2.5% to win their group. Beyond that the path thins quickly, with a quarter-final priced at under 1% and the latter rounds effectively off the table. This is a side the numbers expect to be competitive without ever threatening the favourites.

The form arrives mixed but rarely heavy. Their last ten show plenty of draws — 1-1 results against Wales and Italy in qualifying, a goalless friendly with FYR Macedonia and a 1-1 in Panama — alongside the bigger nights, a 3-1 over Romania and a 6-0 thrashing of San Marino. The lone defeat was a 1-2 home loss to Austria. A squad built on depth in goal, with Jurkas, Vasilj and Zlomislic, and an experienced defensive core including Kolasinac and Katic, looks organised enough to make their group games tight, which is broadly what the 40% survival figure implies.

Algeria.text:
Algeria arrive ranked 28th in the world and carrying recent form that flatters their numbers: a 1-0 win away to the Netherlands in June and a goalless draw with Uruguay in March suggest a side that can live with serious opposition, while the 7-0 demolition of Guatemala shows what happens when they face lesser resistance. The Africa Cup of Nations told a more sober story — four wins, then a 2-0 defeat to Nigeria — and a squad valued at €256.9m, marshalled by defenders like Aït-Nouri and Bensebaïni, looks built to grind rather than overwhelm.

The model reads them as a near-lock to clear the group stage, putting reaching the round of 32 at 84.5%, but it cools sharply after that: winning the group is only a 15.6% shot, the round of 16 sits at 30.9%, and the quarter-finals fade to 15.2%. A title run is priced at just 0.7%, so the realistic ceiling here is a knockout cameo rather than a deep march. The away win in Amsterdam keeps the door ajar, but the numbers say survive first, dream later.

Ghana.text:
Ghana arrive ranked 74th in the world, and the model treats them as long shots: a 3.77% chance of winning their group and just a 9.58% shot at reaching the last 16, with the simulations effectively writing off any deeper run at 0.01% to lift the trophy. The squad is valued at €234.6m, but the recent evidence is uncomfortable. Qualifying went smoothly — four unbeaten across the autumn, including a 5-0 thumping of the Central African Republic and narrow home wins over Mali and Comoros — yet the friendlies that followed told a harsher story.

Against tougher opposition the Black Stars have looked exposed, shipping five to Austria and losing to Germany, Japan, South Korea and Mexico before salvaging a 1-1 draw with Wales in June. That gap between qualifying comfort and friendly struggles is the central question here: the model's near coin-flip survival number (47.36% to clear the group stage into the round of 32) leans on the wins, while the defeats hint the floor could be lower. With the squad's goal-scoring contributions unlisted, the attacking burden is hard to pin down, which only adds to the uncertainty.

Cape Verde.text:
Cape Verde arrive at their first World Cup as one of the tournament's lightest names — 69th in the FIFA ranking, with a squad valued at just €54.5m — but the Tubarões Azuis got here on merit. Their African qualifying campaign carried real weight, beating Cameroon 1-0 at home, winning 2-0 away to Mauritius and 3-0 over Eswatini, with only a 3-3 draw away in Libya spoiling the run. Recent friendlies have kept the mood buoyant: 3-0 wins over both Serbia and Bermuda, plus a 1-1 draw with Finland, though defeats in Chile and away friendlies against Egypt and Iran are a reminder of the level above them.

The model treats this as a tough draw rather than a hopeless one. It gives Cape Verde a 31.1% chance of surviving the group stage and reaching the round of 32, but the road narrows sharply after that — 4.2% to reach the last 16, 0.9% to make the quarter-finals, and a 1.44% shot at actually winning the group. Lift-off beyond a single knockout round looks unlikely, and the champion figure rounds to zero.

Curaçao.text:
Curaçao reached the World Cup the hard way, and the qualifying ledger tells a flattering story: unbeaten across six CONCACAF matches in the dossier, with home wins over Jamaica (2-0) and Bermuda, a 7-0 demolition of Bermuda away, and disciplined goalless draws against Trinidad and Tobago and Jamaica. For a nation ranked 82nd with a squad valued at just €25.78m, getting there at all is the achievement. The warm-up signals, though, were sobering — heavy friendly losses to Scotland (1-4), Australia (1-5) and China (0-2) suggest the step up in class bites hard, even if a 4-0 win over Aruba steadied things before the tournament.

The model treats this as a tournament to survive rather than progress through. It gives Curaçao a 0.48% chance of winning their group and just an 8% shot at reaching the round of 32, with the round of 16 already a long 0.9%. That is the realistic frame: a defensively-organised side, built around three capable goalkeepers and a back line led by Bazoer and Brenet, whose ceiling is a result that embarrasses a bigger name rather than a deep run.

Haiti.text:
Haiti reached the 2026 World Cup the hard way, grinding through CONCACAF qualification with home wins over Costa Rica (1-0) and Nicaragua (2-0) to close out the campaign, after earlier holding Costa Rica to a 3-3 draw away and stifling Honduras to a goalless stalemate at home. The blot was a 0-3 loss in Honduras, a reminder of the ceiling when the defence is stretched. Ranked 83rd in the world with a squad valued at €55.9m, this is a side built on goalkeeping depth and a back line stocked with names like Duverne, Delcroix and Ade rather than marquee attacking talent.

Warm-up form has been mixed: a 4-0 thumping of New Zealand alongside a 1-2 defeat to Peru and a narrow loss to Tunisia. The model sees a tough draw ahead, giving Haiti just a 24.6% chance of surviving the group into the round of 32 and only 4.7% of reaching the last 16. Winning the group sits at 1.4%, and anything beyond the quarter-finals — pegged at 0.9% — looks fanciful. Survival, not a deep run, is the realistic measure of success here.

Iraq.text:
Iraq arrive at their first World Cup in decades the hard way, edging Bolivia 2-1 in April's intercontinental play-off to seal the place after a stubborn Asian qualifying run that included a 0-0 at Saudi Arabia and home and away results against the United Arab Emirates. The form since has been encouraging for a side ranked 57th: a 1-0 friendly win over Andorra and, more eye-catching, a 1-1 draw away to Spain in June. The Lions of Mesopotamia clearly travel well and defend stubbornly, which fits a squad valued at just €21.2m and built around goalkeeping depth and a back line of Putros, Doski and Hussein Ali.

The model is sober about where that gets them. It gives Iraq a 13.5% chance of reaching the round of 32 and only an 0.88% shot at winning their group, with round-of-16 progress priced at 2.4% and anything beyond essentially rounding to zero. That is the realistic ceiling for a team whose strength is keeping games tight rather than winning them. Drawing with Spain shows the floor is higher than the ranking suggests; surviving the group still looks a tall order.

H. Kane.text:
Kane's club output isn't tailing off at 32 — it's accelerating. His 2024 at Bayern was already elite, 26 Bundesliga goals in 31 games plus 11 in 13 Champions League outings. The 2025 campaign topped it: 36 league goals in the same 31 appearances, 14 in 13 in Europe, and a remarkable 10 in six DFB Pokal ties, with ratings climbing to 8.0 in the Champions League and 8.05 in the cup. That is a striker improving his finishing volume rather than coasting on reputation.

It carries straight into England's colours. Eight goals in eight World Cup qualifiers across 2024, then two in two and a 8.1 rating to open the 2026 cycle, suggest the supply line and the form are intact. For a side ranked fourth in the world, that is the profile you want leading the line: a forward scoring at better than a goal a game for his club and converting his international chances just as ruthlessly. The model has every reason to lean on him.

J. David.text:
Jonathan David arrives at a World Cup on his home continent with a club CV that pulls in two directions. The 2024 version was a genuine elite forward: 16 goals in 32 Ligue 1 appearances for Lille at a 7.15 rating, with another nine in Champions League minutes. The 2025 move to Juventus dulled that edge — six Serie A goals across 35 appearances and a rating that slipped to 6.43, with his nine Champions League outings rated a modest 6.30. That's a striker producing less and rated lower in a tougher tactical setting, which tempers expectations of him carrying Canada's attack on volume alone.

The encouraging counterweight is that he keeps scoring in a Canada shirt. Even through the leaner club spell he managed three friendly goals across 2025, and his 2026 sample, though tiny at 169 minutes, shows two goals and a 7.23 rating. At 25 he remains Canada's likeliest finisher, just not the runaway one his Lille numbers once implied.

Mikel Oyarzabal.text:
Oyarzabal arrives in form that is trending up, not coasting. His 2024 La Liga return was steady rather than spectacular — nine goals in 37 appearances at a 6.79 rating — but the 2025 campaign was a genuine step forward: 15 league goals in 34 games at a 7.08 rating, with three more in the Copa del Rey. That is close to a goal every other match from a forward who turns 28 this tournament cycle, squarely in his prime.

The international numbers reinforce it. He scored six in six World Cup qualifiers, chipped in four assists, and has already found the net twice in just 63 minutes of 2026 friendlies, posting an 8.7 rating. For a Spain side ranked second in the world, that combination of rising club output and ruthless qualifying conversion makes him a credible source of goals rather than a passenger. The trajectory points the right way; the question is only how much of that finishing carries into the games that matter most.

Kylian Mbappé.text:
His first Real Madrid season set the tone: 31 goals in 34 La Liga games in 2024, then 25 more in 31 the following campaign, with the Champions League output sharpening rather than fading — 15 goals in just 11 European appearances at an 8.05 rating. That is elite, sustained scoring across two seasons at the highest club level, and it travels: in World Cup qualifying he managed five goals and three assists in only four matches, an 8.38 rating that is among the best on this site.

The one note of caution sits in the 2026 column. Four France friendlies have yielded a single goal across 219 minutes and a 7.03 rating — a small, low-minutes sample, but a quieter read than his club numbers. With France ranked first in the world, the club evidence points to a genuine Golden Boot contender; the recent international form is simply too thin to lean on either way, and worth watching as the tournament nears.

Lautaro Martínez.text:
A note for readers first: the data attached to this page belongs to Emiliano Martínez, Argentina's 33-year-old goalkeeper, not the striker the title suggests, so treat what follows as a goalkeeper's profile.

By that measure the trend is gently downward. His 2024 was the peak: a 7.09 league rating across 37 Premier League games for Aston Villa, climbing to 7.42 in the Champions League and 7.33 over six Copa América appearances. The 2025 campaign cooled to a 6.98 league mark across 32 outings, with the Europa League run (7.12) his steadier work. Into the 2026 cycle, 16 World Cup qualifiers brought a 6.93 rating over 1,459 minutes — solid, but his lowest sustained number here.

The implication for the tournament is a dependable rather than ascendant figure: an established starter behind a third-ranked side, holding a level around 6.9 to 7.0 rather than the 7.3-plus heights of two years ago.

E. Haaland.text:
Haaland's club output has trended upward, not down. At Manchester City he scored 22 in 32 Premier League games across 2024, then raised it to 27 goals and eight assists in 35 league appearances in 2025 — a near-a-goal-a-game striker entering his prime at 25. The Champions League tells the same story: eight goals in ten matches in each of the last two campaigns, proof the level holds against elite defences.

For Norway the numbers are even starker. His 16 goals in eight World Cup qualifying matches in 2024 were the engine that dragged a side ranked 31st toward the finals. The only soft note is recent: two goals in two 2026 qualifiers but a blank, 6.5-rated showing across two friendlies. That is a small sample against weaker stakes, not a warning. On this evidence Haaland arrives as one of the tournament's most reliable scorers, and Norway's ceiling rises and falls almost entirely with how often he finds the net.

Cristiano Ronaldo.text:
At 40, Ronaldo's club output hasn't dipped at all — if anything it's climbing. His Al-Nassr Pro League haul went from 25 goals in 30 games across 2024 to 28 in 30 across 2025, the kind of penalty-box scoring rate that travels regardless of league quality. The 7.16 league rating in 2025 sits a touch below 2024's 7.53, hinting his all-round game is narrowing toward pure finishing, but the goals keep coming.

For Portugal that profile is double-edged. He buried eight in eight Nations League outings and five in five World Cup qualifiers, so the international tap is still on. Yet his most recent appearance, a 45-minute friendly cameo rated 6.5, is a reminder that minutes management is now part of the picture. Expect Portugal, ranked fifth in the world, to feed him chances and lean on his record, while accepting he may be a sharp half-game weapon rather than a 90-minute fixture this summer.

R. Jiménez.text:
At 34, Raúl Jiménez is leaning on the gentle downward slope of his Fulham output rather than falling off it. His 2024 was the high-water mark: 12 Premier League goals from 38 games at a 7.01 rating, the kind of reliable centre-forward season that anchors a side. The 2025 campaign cooled to nine goals in 36 league matches and a 6.68 rating, still useful production but a clear step down in both finishing and overall influence. That drift matters for a tournament summer, because a year-on-year decline at his age rarely reverses on its own.

What keeps him interesting is the Mexico shirt. The same player who looked ordinary in club friendlies in 2025 (6.77) produced five goals in three Nations League games in 2024 at an 8.77 rating. The international version of Jiménez has historically outscored the club version, so treat his Fulham numbers as a floor rather than a ceiling — but expect a supporting scorer now, not a talisman.

L. Messi.text:
At 38, Lionel Messi's club output hasn't dipped — it's climbed. His 2024 MLS season already read 21 goals and 11 assists in 22 games at an 8.33 rating, and 2025 was better still: 35 goals and 23 assists across 31 matches with a remarkable 8.60 rating. The 2026 picture, on a smaller sample, holds the line — 13 goals and seven assists in 14 league games at 8.67 — and notably he's been logged as a midfielder now rather than a pure attacker, a sign he's dictating play as much as finishing it.

That production has carried into the national team. Across South American qualification he posted eight goals in 12 appearances at an 8.03 rating, the kind of return that keeps a third-ranked Argentina among the genuine contenders. The implication for the tournament is straightforward: there's no statistical evidence of decline to lean on here. If anything, the numbers argue the model should treat him as a primary goal source rather than a passenger.

Brahim Díaz.text:
A Real Madrid career in miniature: useful but rarely central. His 2024 La Liga return of four goals and two assists across 31 appearances and just 1,405 minutes told the story of a rotation forward, and 2025 sharpened it — one goal but six assists in 30 league games, with his rating drifting from 6.86 to 6.68 as his minutes shrank. The Champions League work mirrors that, a goal apiece across both seasons off limited time.

What changes everything is the shirt. For Morocco, Díaz is a different player: seven goals in four Africa Cup of Nations qualifiers at an 8.22 rating, then five more across seven finals matches, where he was deployed as an attacker rather than a squad midfielder. That's the version, freed and central, that matters here. The caveat is recent: just one goal in four friendlies through 2026 at a modest 6.88. So the ceiling is real and proven at international level, but the form line into the tournament is quieter than the headline numbers suggest.

M. Olise.text:
Olise's trajectory at Bayern reads like a player still climbing. His debut 2024 Bundesliga season already delivered 12 goals and 15 assists at a 7.92 rating, and rather than regressing he kicked on in 2025 — 15 goals and 19 assists across 32 league games, a direct involvement in 34 goals from one campaign, plus five goals and six assists in the Champions League. The rating barely moved (7.89), which tells you this is a sustained level, not a hot streak.

That matters for France, the world's top-ranked side. He has carried the form into a national-team shirt: two goals at an 8.42 rating across World Cup qualifying, and three goals with an assist in just 234 friendly minutes this year. A 24-year-old producing a goal contribution roughly every other game at club level, and finishing sharply in early-2026 internationals, arrives as one of the tournament's most reliable creators rather than a hopeful breakout. The numbers point one direction.

F. Wirtz.text:
Wirtz arrives at the tournament carrying two very different club stories. The 2024 version, in Bayer Leverkusen colours, was a creative engine: 10 goals and 12 assists across 31 Bundesliga games at a 7.7 rating, plus six Champions League goals in nine outings. His first Liverpool season reads more muted — 5 goals and 3 assists in 33 Premier League appearances at 6.81, a clear step down from the numbers that built his reputation, with the Champions League return (1 goal, 3 assists at 7.19) softening the picture only slightly.

The encouraging signal for Germany is the most recent one: across four 2026 friendlies he has 3 goals and 2 assists at a 7.95 rating, his sharpest output in a national shirt. At 22 he profiles as a genuine difference-maker if that international form holds. The honest read is that his club ceiling is higher than last season showed, and whether the Leverkusen or the Liverpool Wirtz turns up will shape how far his end product carries Germany.

B. Embolo.text:
Embolo's club output has trended the right way at the most relevant moment. His 2024 was a steady Monaco season — six goals and four assists across 29 Ligue 1 starts, plus a creative Champions League cameo of one goal and three assists, but a modest 6.75 rating. The 2025 switch to Rennes sharpened the goalscoring: nine Ligue 1 goals in 31 games, his best return in the dossier, even if the 6.71 rating suggests the all-round contribution stayed level rather than spiking.

What lifts the tournament read is his Switzerland form, which consistently outpaces his club numbers. He scored four in six World Cup qualifiers (7.23), three in four 2025 friendlies (7.05), and two more in three 2026 friendlies at a 7.27 rating. That pattern — a reliable rather than prolific club striker who reliably finds the net in national colours — makes him a credible focal point for a 19th-ranked Swiss side, and a sensible name in the anytime-scorer markets.

V. Gyökeres.text:
The two-season split tells a clear story. In 2024 at Sporting CP, Gyökeres was a goal machine — 39 in 33 Primeira Liga games at a 7.99 rating, plus six in eight Champions League outings. The 2025 move to Arsenal brought an inevitable recalibration: 14 goals in 36 Premier League appearances and a 6.62 rating, with five more across 13 Champions League games. That's still useful output, but a different volume entirely against stiffer defences.

For Sweden the read is more encouraging. He scored nine in six Nations League matches in 2024 at a standout 8.65 rating, added four in World Cup qualifying, and has already opened his 2026 account with a goal in his only friendly. The honest take: his Arsenal numbers caution against expecting Sporting-era hauls, but his international scoring rate has held up regardless of club context. Backing a 38th-ranked side, he remains Sweden's clearest route to goals — just temper the ceiling.

L. Díaz.text:
Díaz arrives at the World Cup in the best vein of his career. His last Liverpool season was solid without being spectacular — 13 goals in 36 Premier League games at a 7.27 rating — but the move to Bayern München lifted him to another level. Across 2025 he posted 15 goals and 14 assists in 32 Bundesliga matches, and was even sharper in Europe, with seven goals and three assists in 12 Champions League outings at a 7.52 rating, his best mark anywhere. That is a player no longer just a finisher but a genuine creator.

For Colombia the read is encouraging. He carried that form into qualifying, scoring seven and assisting three across 17 CONMEBOL games at a 7.25 rating, often operating in a deeper midfield role. At 28 he is squarely in his peak, and a side ranked 13th in the world will lean heavily on a forward producing at both ends of the pitch.

S. Mané.text:
Mané's Al-Nassr output tells a clear story of a forward gently winding down rather than dropping off a cliff. His 2024 Pro League season was his peak: 14 goals and 11 assists across 32 games at a 7.56 rating, a genuine double-figure return in both columns. In 2025 the numbers softened to 10 goals and six assists from 25 league games at 7.28, with his listed role drifting from attacker to midfielder — a deeper brief that thins the goal tally but keeps him involved.

That positional shift carried into the Africa Cup of Nations, where he played midfield and still chipped in two goals and three assists at 7.31. At 33 he is more creator than out-and-out poacher now, but the early-2026 signal is loud: two goals in a single 60-minute friendly for Senegal at an 8.9 rating. For a side ranked 14th, that points to a player still capable of deciding matches, just from a slightly less explosive base than two seasons ago.

Raphinha.text:
Raphinha arrives at this World Cup off the back of a genuinely elite club peak. His 2024 was the standout: 18 goals and nine assists in La Liga at a 7.8 rating, plus a remarkable 13 goals and eight assists in just 14 Champions League games, where his 8.25 rating was the best of any line on his card. The 2025 season saw a tactical shift, deployed more as a midfielder, and the raw output cooled slightly — 13 league goals in 22 appearances at 7.55, with the Champions League return dropping to three goals from seven games at 7.31.

For Brazil he has carried that into qualifying: five goals and two assists across 13 World Cup qualifiers at a 7.42 rating. The one flag is friendlies, where he has consistently dipped — 6.7 in 2024, 6.6 in 2026. The pattern suggests a player who delivers when the games matter, which is exactly what Brazil will need.

Vinícius Júnior.text:
Vinícius arrives off two strong club seasons but with a softer recent dip worth watching. His 2024 was the Champions League peak — eight goals in twelve appearances at a 7.56 rating, alongside 11 goals and eight assists in La Liga. The 2025 campaign pushed his scoring further, 16 league goals across 36 games at a 7.51 rating, though his Champions League returns leveled to five goals and five assists.

The flag is in the Brazil shirt. World Cup qualifying brought just two goals and one assist in 11 appearances, and his rating slipped to 6.93 — his lowest sustained run in the dossier, often deployed in midfield rather than as an out-and-out attacker. The pattern is clear: devastating in club colours, more muted for the national team. For a Brazil side ranked sixth, his ceiling is among the tournament's highest, but the qualifying numbers suggest the goals haven't reliably translated, and that gap is the open question heading into 2026.

K. De Bruyne.text:
At 34, Kevin De Bruyne has been quietly reinventing himself by season. His final Manchester City campaign in 2024 read like a player in transition — 4 goals and 7 assists across 33 Premier League games at a 7.32 rating, but a muted 6.86 in the Champions League suggested the biggest nights were getting harder. The move to Napoli has sharpened the goal threat: 5 goals in just 18 Serie A appearances, a higher per-minute return even if the 7.04 rating sits slightly below his City baseline, plus two assists in three Champions League outings.

The arc points to a player trading volume of creation for cleaner end product, which matters less in a tournament where Belgium will run through him regardless. The early signs in 2026 are encouraging — a goal and a 7.33 across four friendlies. For a ninth-ranked side, that's a creative spine still producing; the question is whether the legs hold up over a long summer.

Son Heung-Min.text:
Son's trajectory tells a clear story. His final Tottenham league season in 2024 was steady rather than spectacular — seven goals and nine assists across 30 appearances at a 7.26 rating. The move to Los Angeles FC reignited him: 12 goals in just 11 MLS games during 2025, an 8.18 rating that ranks as the standout number on his page. The 2026 club picture is murkier — zero league goals in 13 MLS outings, though eight assists and a 7.15 rating show he's still creating, just not finishing.

The encouraging signal for South Korea is that his international output hasn't dipped. Across World Cup qualifying he posted seven goals and four assists in 13 games at a 7.81 rating, his best competitive return anywhere recently. At 33, the club goal drought is worth watching, but a player who scores freely in a Korea shirt is exactly what they need. He arrives as the focal point of a side ranked 25th in the world, and on national-team form he's still delivering.

Bruno Fernandes.text:
At 31, Bruno Fernandes arrives in elite creative form rather than fading. His 2025 Premier League return is the standout line: nine goals and 21 assists across 35 appearances, a jump from the eight goals and ten assists he managed in 2024. That doubling of his assist output points to a player whose game is tilting from finisher to provider, which travels well to a tournament where Portugal will often dominate possession and need someone to unlock packed defences.

The 2024 Europa League run reinforces the case — seven goals in 14 games at an 8.05 rating, his best sustained number in the dossier. He has stayed durable too, logging over 3,000 league minutes in each of the last two seasons. The early 2026 sample is thin, a goal and an assist across three friendlies at a 7.5 rating, so we lean on the club body of work. For a side ranked fifth in the world, that profile makes him their most reliable source of chance creation.

S. Rahimi.text:
Rahimi's club graph points the wrong way heading into the tournament. In 2024 he was sharp for Al Ain, eight goals in 18 Pro League games at a 7.44 rating and a ruthless five in seven AFC Champions League Elite outings (7.54). The 2025 club return is thinner: two goals in 15 Pro League appearances, the rating down to 6.98, and a blank across three Club World Cup games at 6.85. His most recent club season, then, reads more like a steady starter than the matchwinner of a year earlier.

The caveat is that international Rahimi has kept scoring while club Rahimi cooled — five goals in three Africa Cup of Nations games and three in five AFCON qualifiers. For an eighth-ranked Morocco, that split is the open question: the dossier shows a player who lifts for his country even as the league numbers fade, so treat his finishing as live without leaning on the recent club form to project it.

C. Gakpo.text:
Gakpo's club output has cooled even as his minutes have climbed. In 2024 he was a genuine scoring threat for Liverpool, ten Premier League goals and four assists at a 7.22 rating, supplemented by five in the League Cup and three in the Champions League. The 2025 season tells a different story: 2,765 league minutes but only seven goals and five assists, his rating sliding to 6.89, with the productive cup hauls drying up. A reshuffle from attacker to midfielder explains some of that, but the raw return is plainly thinner.

The encouraging counterweight is what he does in orange. His 2024 World Cup qualifying campaign was excellent, four goals and four assists across eight games at an 8.06 rating, and he has carried that into 2026 with two goals in four friendlies and a 7.85 rating. The pattern suggests a player whose club numbers undersell his international ceiling, which is the version the Netherlands will be counting on this summer.

M. Taremi.text:
Taremi's last two seasons tell a story of revival. At Inter in 2024 he was a peripheral figure — 26 Serie A appearances stretched across just 816 minutes for a single goal, with a modest 6.7 rating, and even his sharper Champions League work (one goal and three assists at 7.08) came in fragments. The 2025 move to Olympiakos restored him to a regular starter and a genuine focal point: 10 goals in 24 Super League games at 7.07, plus two more in the Champions League. That points to a forward who needs minutes to matter, and now gets them.

For the tournament, the more relevant number is in an Iran shirt. Across World Cup qualifying he posted five goals and four assists in 15 games at a standout 7.76 rating — comfortably his best return anywhere. A 33-year-old who is trusted, central and on the ball for his country is exactly the profile that travels into a major finals.

A. Ueda.text:
Ueda's club trajectory points one way: up. At Feyenoord his 2024 Eredivisie return was a steady seven goals in 21 league games at a 6.83 rating, useful rather than prolific. The 2025 season is where he turned into a genuine number nine, hammering 25 goals in 31 Eredivisie matches at a 7.17 rating — a near-fourfold jump on the previous campaign. The caveat is level: he managed only one goal in six Europa League outings and none in his Champions League minutes, so the elite-defence question is fair.

For 2026 that caveat matters less than the form he carries in. Eight goals in nine Asian qualifiers at a 7.49 rating is the sharpest he has looked, and the international scoring has plainly outrun his European club ceiling. Three blank friendlies (6.37) are a small flag, but a striker peaking at 27 and finishing everything in qualification is exactly the profile Japan want leading the line.

Lamine Yamal.text:
At 18, Lamine Yamal's club trajectory points one way: up. His 2024 La Liga return of nine goals and 13 assists across 2,771 minutes was already elite for his age, but the 2025 campaign saw the output sharpen into something more dangerous — 16 goals and 11 assists in 2,268 league minutes, a near doubling of his scoring rate, with his match rating climbing from 7.79 to 7.95. The Champions League tells the same story: six goals, four assists and an 8.14 rating across ten games, his strongest stage yet. That is a player adding end product without losing creation, which is exactly the profile that travels to a World Cup with the world's second-ranked side.

The one note of caution is recent: two Spain friendlies in 2026 brought no goals or assists and a flatter 7.1. It's a tiny sample against meaningful club evidence, but worth watching for whether the scoring surge carries into the international window.

Pedri.text:
At 23, Pedri carries two seasons of metronomic Barcelona form into the tournament. His 2024 La Liga numbers — 36 appearances, 2,808 minutes, four goals and five assists at a 7.62 rating — gave way to a 2025 campaign that traded goals for creation: two goals but nine assists across 29 league games, the rating barely dipping to 7.54. That's a midfielder whose output is shifting toward supply rather than finishing, which suits Spain's possession game and a side ranked second in the world.

The flag is volume. He stayed durable through 2024 but logged fewer minutes in 2025, and his 2026 international sample is thin — three friendly caps, 90 minutes, a modest 7.1. Encouragingly, when he has worn the national shirt for stakes, he has delivered: a standout 8.5 across four World Cup qualifiers with two goals. The form points to a creative hub in good rhythm; the open question is workload, not quality.

Vitinha.text:
Vitinha has built two highly consistent seasons at the heart of Paris Saint-Germain, and the ratings tell the story before the goals do. In 2024 he posted a 7.52 in Ligue 1 and a near-identical 7.56 across 17 Champions League appearances, chipping in five league goals. The 2025 campaign saw the output shift rather than fade: just one league goal but seven assists at a 7.58 rating, while his Champions League contribution jumped to six goals from 17 matches at the same 7.58 mark. That is a midfielder trusted with heavy minutes — over 2,000 in Ligue 1 each year — and delivering at elite level in both phases of play.

For a Portugal side ranked fifth in the world, that reliability matters. He rarely dips below a 7.2 in any competition, and his three Nations League assists hint at the creative role he can fill. The form points to a player arriving at the tournament settled, durable and in genuinely top condition.

Ousmane Dembélé.text:
Dembélé arrives off the most productive stretch of his career. The 2024 season was the breakout: 21 goals and seven assists in 30 Ligue 1 games at a 7.72 rating, with another eight goals and six assists across 15 Champions League outings. The 2025 numbers cooled slightly without collapsing — 10 goals and seven assists in just 22 Ligue 1 appearances, plus eight more in Europe, his rating easing to 7.41 and 7.16. That is still elite output, and the shift from pure winger to a more central scoring threat at PSG is the story worth tracking.

The caution sits with France. His 2026 friendly minutes show no goals and a single assist across two games at 6.95, deployed in midfield rather than as an attacker. With the world's top-ranked side, the question is whether his club finishing translates when he is moved off the touchline. The club form says he should be a genuine goal source; the early international signs ask us to wait and see.

Rayan Cherki.text:
At 22, Cherki arrives off a two-season climb that should reassure France's staff. His 2024 at Lyon was the breakout: eight goals and 11 assists across 30 Ligue 1 games at a 7.63 rating, with another four goals and eight assists in the Europa League at an even better 8.02. The move to Manchester City in 2025 took the goals down but kept the creation flowing — four goals and a healthy 12 assists in 33 Premier League appearances, plus a useful return in the cups, including three goals in both the League Cup and the Champions League.

The pattern is a playmaker whose end product travels well across leagues and competitions, which matters for a France side ranked top in the world. The early 2026 sample is small — one goal in three friendlies at 7.27 — so the tournament case rests on his assist numbers rather than scoring. As a supply line into a deep French attack, the evidence points the right way.

Jude Bellingham.text:
Bellingham's club output has cooled rather than collapsed. His first full Real Madrid season in 2024 set a high bar: 9 goals and 8 assists across 31 La Liga games at a 7.48 rating, backed by 3 goals and 3 assists in the Champions League. The 2025 campaign reads a step down — 6 goals and 4 assists in 28 league appearances, with the rating slipping to 7.28 and his Champions League mark easing to 7.2. That's still strong production from midfield, but the goal involvement per minute has clearly thinned.

The more encouraging signal for the tournament comes in an England shirt. He posted an 8.33 rating across four Nations League outings when pushed into a more advanced attacking role in 2024, and his early 2026 World Cup qualifying form is bright at 7.6 with an assist. England rank fourth in the world, and a Bellingham deployed higher up the pitch has historically been their most dangerous version of him.

Unai Simón.text:
Spain's number one arrives at the World Cup as the settled, played-every-week option, and the workload backs that up: 37 La Liga appearances and 3,330 minutes across the 2025 season for Athletic Club, up from 25 games and 1,890 minutes the year before. That's a goalkeeper trusted with the full grind of a domestic campaign rather than rotated.

The form trend is steady rather than rising.
